trade.summary: China is Canada's second-largest trading partner but the relationship is under severe strain. Canada exports canola, pork, seafood, and natural resources to China. China exports manufactured goods, electronics, and consumer products to Canada. Trade flourished before political tensions but is increasingly complicated by security concerns.
disputes: China banned Canadian canola in 2019 (later partially lifted)

diplomatic.summary: Canada-China relations are severely damaged. The arrest of Meng Wanzhou (Huawei CFO) in 2018 and China's retaliatory detention of Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor (the two Michaels) poisoned relations for years. Canada has since banned Huawei from its 5G network, joined AUKUS-adjacent security frameworks, and increased China-critical rhetoric. Relations remain cold despite the 2021 resolution of the Meng/Michaels cases.

military.summary: No military cooperation. Canada participates in Five Eyes intelligence sharing about China. Canada has increased military presence in Indo-Pacific as part of China-containment posture. No arms transfers.
incidents: Chinese naval vessels near Canadian Arctic exercises

financial.summary: Canada has increased scrutiny of Chinese investment through the Investment Canada Act. Several Chinese state-linked acquisitions have been blocked. Chinese investors still hold Canadian mining and real estate assets but new investment faces political barriers.

energy.summary: China imports Canadian LNG (as projects come online) and some crude oil via the Trans Mountain Expansion. Canada sees China as a diversification market away from US energy dependency. Energy trade has continued despite political tensions.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation has contracted significantly under security concerns. Canada has increased scrutiny of research collaboration involving Chinese nationals and institutions, particularly in sensitive technology areas.
